evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
Entity of type FAQPage has no @id — cross-page interconnection blocked
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs
Primary keyword "q1 health check" not in first 8 words of TL;DR
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 6/10 — The GP voice appears in flashes (Ramadan quips, 45°C car park) but FAQ and summary sections revert to generic content-marketing prose.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — Some vivid lines (45°C car park, January-self vs July-self) sit next to heavy boilerplate phrases.
Quotable sentences 6/10 — A few quotable zingers exist, but they're buried among flat clinical summaries nobody would repeat.
Intro earns attention 4/10 — Opens with a definitional TL;DR and duplicated title; the strong GP line is buried several sentences down.

specificity 6/10 — The post has strong specific moments (AED 650 panel, 48-hour results, 40% adherence improvement, 1.5–2L water, 6 am vs 6 pm walk, HbA1c/LDL/vitamin D/TSH named explicitly). However, several key sentences drift into abstraction without anchoring data: 'genuinely change outcomes for chronic conditions' names no condition, no number, no source. Similarly, 'Small changes compound fast' and 'Frequent check-ins mean you adapt strategies before motivation or context shifts' are generic self-help phrasing. The identified sentence is the clearest offender — it asserts a claim about chronic conditions vs fitness tracking with zero specificity about which conditions, what outcomes, or by how much.
